Today, "Shimotsukeno" is considered the correct pronunciation (see {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野#「毛野」の由来と読み}}).</ref>) is a [[Japanese clan]] that derives its name from the region of Shimotsukeno. This [[imperial clan]] (皇別氏族) claimed..."
revision.text:
'''Shimotsukeno clan''' (下毛野氏)
The '''Shimotsukeno clan''' (しもつけのうじ, しもつけぬうじ<ref group="注">From the Edo period until recently, "Shimotsukeno" was also referred to as "Shimotsukenu." Today, "Shimotsukeno" is considered the correct pronunciation (see {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野#「毛野」の由来と読み}}).</ref>) is a [[Japanese clan]] that derives its name from the region of Shimotsukeno.
This [[imperial clan]] (皇別氏族) claimed descent from {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, a [[prince]] of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. The clan initially used the title "Shimotsukeno no Kimi" (下毛野君) or "Shimotsukeno no Kō" (下毛野公), later adopting the title "Shimotsukeno no Ason" (下毛野朝臣).
== Overview ==
After the [[Taika Reforms]], the Shimotsukeno clan became a mid-tier noble family. By the [[10th century]], they were active as low-ranking military officers within the [[imperial court]]. The clan is mentioned in early chronicles as serving the court, but their activities before the Taika Reforms remain unclear. As their name implies, the clan originated in the Shimotsukeno region (modern-day southwestern [[Tochigi Prefecture]]), though historical evidence is sparse.
The name "{{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}}" refers to an ancient region encompassing modern-day [[Gunma Prefecture]] and southwestern Tochigi Prefecture. The area closer to the capital was called "{{ill|Kamitsukeno|ja|上毛野}}" (later {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}), while the more distant part was termed "{{ill|Shimotsukeno|ja|下毛野}}" (later {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}).
== Origins ==
=== Historical Records ===
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} ordered his son {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}} to govern the eastern provinces. It also identifies Toyokiiribiko as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and Shimotsukeno clan.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Sujin, year 48.</ref> This lineage is similarly recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.<ref group="原">Kojiki, Emperor Sujin's record.</ref>
The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} of {{ill|815|ja|弘仁6年}} states that the Shimotsukeno no Ason descended from Toyokiiribiko.<ref group="原">Shinsen Shōjiroku, Sakyo Imperial Lineage, Shimotsukeno no Ason.</ref> Other clans claiming descent from Toyokiiribiko include {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}, {{ill|Ōno clan|ja|大野氏}}, and {{ill|Kuramochi clan|ja|車持氏}}, collectively known as the {{ill|Tōgoku Six Clan Ason|ja|東国六腹朝臣}}.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Enryaku 10, 4th month.</ref>
The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that in {{ill|707|ja|慶雲4年}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} changed the clan name to "Shimotsukeno Kawachi no Ason," indicating a connection to the Kawachi District.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Keiun 4, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
=== Archaeological Evidence ===
[[File:Azuma-kofun zenkei.JPG|thumb|210px|right|{{ill|Azuma Kofun|ja|吾妻古墳}} in Tochigi Prefecture.]] Numerous {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} burial mounds were constructed in the Keno region during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. In the Shimotsukeno area, the late 5th and early 6th centuries saw the construction of large kofun such as {{ill|Marishitenzuka Kofun|ja|摩利支天塚古墳}} (121 meters) and {{ill|Biwasuka Kofun|ja|琵琶塚古墳}} (123 meters).<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref>
The unique "Shimotsuke-type Kofun," characterized by stone chambers in the anterior part and mounds on elevated platforms, are believed to be the tombs of the Shimotsukeno clan.<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref> Nearby, the ruins of the {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}—the clan's temple—further demonstrate their historical significance.<ref>See {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji Ruins|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}.</ref>
== History ==
In {{ill|684|ja|天武天皇13年}}, Emperor Tenmu conferred the title "Ason" on the Shimotsukeno clan as part of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Nobility|ja|八色の姓}}.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Tenmu, year 13, 11th month.</ref>
Notable members of the clan include {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}}, who contributed to drafting the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, and {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}}, a poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.<ref>{{Sfn|国史大辞典編集委員会|1986|p=|loc=§. 下毛野氏}}</ref> Over time, the clan's influence waned, and they served as middle- and low-ranking court officials.<ref>{{Sfn|kotobank-下毛野氏|loc=世界大百科事典}}</ref>
== Notable Members == * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}} - Compiler of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} - Deputy general in the conquest of the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}} - Poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="注"}}
'''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="原"}}
'''Citations''' {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}} * {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} * {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}
[[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Imperial clans]] [[Category:Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Shimotsukeno clan]] [[ja:下毛野氏]]

== Origins ==
=== Historical Records ===
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} ordered his son {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}} to govern the eastern provinces. It also identifies Toyokiiribiko as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and Shimotsukeno clan.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Sujin, year 48.</ref> This lineage is similarly recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.<ref group="原">Kojiki, Emperor Sujin's record.</ref>
The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} of {{ill|815|ja|弘仁6年}} states that the Shimotsukeno no Ason descended from Toyokiiribiko.<ref group="原">Shinsen Shōjiroku, Sakyo Imperial Lineage, Shimotsukeno no Ason.</ref> Other clans claiming descent from Toyokiiribiko include {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}, {{ill|Ōno clan|ja|大野氏}}, and {{ill|Kuramochi clan|ja|車持氏}}, collectively known as the {{ill|Tōgoku Six Clan Ason|ja|東国六腹朝臣}}.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Enryaku 10, 4th month.</ref>
The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that in {{ill|707|ja|慶雲4年}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} changed the clan name to "Shimotsukeno Kawachi no Ason," indicating a connection to the Kawachi District.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Keiun 4, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
=== Archaeological Evidence ===
[[File:Azuma-kofun zenkei.JPG|thumb|210px|right|{{ill|Azuma Kofun|ja|吾妻古墳}} in Tochigi Prefecture.]] Numerous {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} burial mounds were constructed in the Keno region during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. In the Shimotsukeno area, the late 5th and early 6th centuries saw the construction of large kofun such as {{ill|Marishitenzuka Kofun|ja|摩利支天塚古墳}} (121 meters) and {{ill|Biwasuka Kofun|ja|琵琶塚古墳}} (123 meters).<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref>
The unique "Shimotsuke-type Kofun," characterized by stone chambers in the anterior part and mounds on elevated platforms, are believed to be the tombs of the Shimotsukeno clan.<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref> Nearby, the ruins of the {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}—the clan's temple—further demonstrate their historical significance.<ref>See {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji Ruins|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}.</ref>
== History ==
In {{ill|684|ja|天武天皇13年}}, Emperor Tenmu conferred the title "Ason" on the Shimotsukeno clan as part of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Nobility|ja|八色の姓}}.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Tenmu, year 13, 11th month.</ref>
Notable members of the clan include {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}}, who contributed to drafting the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, and {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}}, a poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.<ref>{{Sfn|国史大辞典編集委員会|1986|p=|loc=§. 下毛野氏}}</ref> Over time, the clan's influence waned, and they served as middle- and low-ranking court officials.<ref>{{Sfn|kotobank-下毛野氏|loc=世界大百科事典}}</ref>
== Notable Members == * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}} - Compiler of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} - Deputy general in the conquest of the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}} - Poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="注"}}
'''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="原"}}
'''Citations''' {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}} * {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} * {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}

revision.comment: deciding to rename this category to a more descriptive one
revision.text:
'''Shimotsukeno clan''' (下毛野氏)
The '''Shimotsukeno clan''' (しもつけのうじ, しもつけぬうじ<ref group="注">From the Edo period until recently, "Shimotsukeno" was also referred to as "Shimotsukenu." Today, "Shimotsukeno" is considered the correct pronunciation (see {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野#「毛野」の由来と読み}}).</ref>) is a [[Japanese clan]] that derives its name from the region of Shimotsukeno.
This [[imperial clan]] (皇別氏族) claimed descent from {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, a [[prince]] of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. The clan initially used the title "Shimotsukeno no Kimi" (下毛野君) or "Shimotsukeno no Kō" (下毛野公), later adopting the title "Shimotsukeno no Ason" (下毛野朝臣).
== Overview ==
After the [[Taika Reforms]], the Shimotsukeno clan became a mid-tier noble family. By the [[10th century]], they were active as low-ranking military officers within the [[imperial court]]. The clan is mentioned in early chronicles as serving the court, but their activities before the Taika Reforms remain unclear. As their name implies, the clan originated in the Shimotsukeno region (modern-day southwestern [[Tochigi Prefecture]]), though historical evidence is sparse.
The name "{{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}}" refers to an ancient region encompassing modern-day [[Gunma Prefecture]] and southwestern Tochigi Prefecture. The area closer to the capital was called "{{ill|Kamitsukeno|ja|上毛野}}" (later {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}), while the more distant part was termed "{{ill|Shimotsukeno|ja|下毛野}}" (later {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}).
== Origins ==
=== Historical Records ===
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} ordered his son {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}} to govern the eastern provinces. It also identifies Toyokiiribiko as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and Shimotsukeno clan.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Sujin, year 48.</ref> This lineage is similarly recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.<ref group="原">Kojiki, Emperor Sujin's record.</ref>
The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} of {{ill|815|ja|弘仁6年}} states that the Shimotsukeno no Ason descended from Toyokiiribiko.<ref group="原">Shinsen Shōjiroku, Sakyo Imperial Lineage, Shimotsukeno no Ason.</ref> Other clans claiming descent from Toyokiiribiko include {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}, {{ill|Ōno clan|ja|大野氏}}, and {{ill|Kuramochi clan|ja|車持氏}}, collectively known as the {{ill|Tōgoku Six Clan Ason|ja|東国六腹朝臣}}.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Enryaku 10, 4th month.</ref>
The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that in {{ill|707|ja|慶雲4年}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} changed the clan name to "Shimotsukeno Kawachi no Ason," indicating a connection to the Kawachi District.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Keiun 4, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
=== Archaeological Evidence ===
[[File:Azuma-kofun zenkei.JPG|thumb|210px|right|{{ill|Azuma Kofun|ja|吾妻古墳}} in Tochigi Prefecture.]] Numerous {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} burial mounds were constructed in the Keno region during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. In the Shimotsukeno area, the late 5th and early 6th centuries saw the construction of large kofun such as {{ill|Marishitenzuka Kofun|ja|摩利支天塚古墳}} (121 meters) and {{ill|Biwasuka Kofun|ja|琵琶塚古墳}} (123 meters).<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref>
The unique "Shimotsuke-type Kofun," characterized by stone chambers in the anterior part and mounds on elevated platforms, are believed to be the tombs of the Shimotsukeno clan.<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref> Nearby, the ruins of the {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}—the clan's temple—further demonstrate their historical significance.<ref>See {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji Ruins|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}.</ref>
== History ==
In {{ill|684|ja|天武天皇13年}}, Emperor Tenmu conferred the title "Ason" on the Shimotsukeno clan as part of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Nobility|ja|八色の姓}}.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Tenmu, year 13, 11th month.</ref>
Notable members of the clan include {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}}, who contributed to drafting the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, and {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}}, a poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.<ref>{{Sfn|国史大辞典編集委員会|1986|p=|loc=§. 下毛野氏}}</ref> Over time, the clan's influence waned, and they served as middle- and low-ranking court officials.<ref>{{Sfn|kotobank-下毛野氏|loc=世界大百科事典}}</ref>
== Notable Members == * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}} - Compiler of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} - Deputy general in the conquest of the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}} - Poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.

[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Imperial clans]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Shimotsukeno clan]] [[ja:下毛野氏]]

'''Shimotsukeno clan''' (下毛野氏)
The '''Shimotsukeno clan''' (しもつけのうじ, しもつけぬうじ<ref group="注">From the Edo period until recently, "Shimotsukeno" was also referred to as "Shimotsukenu." Today, "Shimotsukeno" is considered the correct pronunciation (see {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野#「毛野」の由来と読み}}).</ref>) is a [[Japanese clan]] that derives its name from the region of Shimotsukeno.
This [[imperial clan]] (皇別氏族) claimed descent from {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, a [[prince]] of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. The clan initially used the title "Shimotsukeno no Kimi" (下毛野君) or "Shimotsukeno no Kō" (下毛野公), later adopting the title "Shimotsukeno no Ason" (下毛野朝臣).
== Overview ==
After the [[Taika Reforms]], the Shimotsukeno clan became a mid-tier noble family. By the [[10th century]], they were active as low-ranking military officers within the [[imperial court]]. The clan is mentioned in early chronicles as serving the court, but their activities before the Taika Reforms remain unclear. As their name implies, the clan originated in the Shimotsukeno region (modern-day southwestern [[Tochigi Prefecture]]), though historical evidence is sparse.
The name "{{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}}" refers to an ancient region encompassing modern-day [[Gunma Prefecture]] and southwestern Tochigi Prefecture. The area closer to the capital was called "{{ill|Kamitsukeno|ja|上毛野}}" (later {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}), while the more distant part was termed "{{ill|Shimotsukeno|ja|下毛野}}" (later {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}).
== Origins ==
=== Historical Records ===
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} ordered his son {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}} to govern the eastern provinces. It also identifies Toyokiiribiko as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and Shimotsukeno clan.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Sujin, year 48.</ref> This lineage is similarly recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.<ref group="原">Kojiki, Emperor Sujin's record.</ref>
The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} of {{ill|815|ja|弘仁6年}} states that the Shimotsukeno no Ason descended from Toyokiiribiko.<ref group="原">Shinsen Shōjiroku, Sakyo Imperial Lineage, Shimotsukeno no Ason.</ref> Other clans claiming descent from Toyokiiribiko include {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}, {{ill|Ōno clan|ja|大野氏}}, and {{ill|Kuramochi clan|ja|車持氏}}, collectively known as the {{ill|Tōgoku Six Clan Ason|ja|東国六腹朝臣}}.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Enryaku 10, 4th month.</ref>
The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that in {{ill|707|ja|慶雲4年}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} changed the clan name to "Shimotsukeno Kawachi no Ason," indicating a connection to the Kawachi District.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Keiun 4, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
=== Archaeological Evidence ===
[[File:Azuma-kofun zenkei.JPG|thumb|210px|right|{{ill|Azuma Kofun|ja|吾妻古墳}} in Tochigi Prefecture.]] Numerous {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} burial mounds were constructed in the Keno region during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. In the Shimotsukeno area, the late 5th and early 6th centuries saw the construction of large kofun such as {{ill|Marishitenzuka Kofun|ja|摩利支天塚古墳}} (121 meters) and {{ill|Biwasuka Kofun|ja|琵琶塚古墳}} (123 meters).<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref>
The unique "Shimotsuke-type Kofun," characterized by stone chambers in the anterior part and mounds on elevated platforms, are believed to be the tombs of the Shimotsukeno clan.<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref> Nearby, the ruins of the {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}—the clan's temple—further demonstrate their historical significance.<ref>See {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji Ruins|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}.</ref>
== History ==
In {{ill|684|ja|天武天皇13年}}, Emperor Tenmu conferred the title "Ason" on the Shimotsukeno clan as part of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Nobility|ja|八色の姓}}.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Tenmu, year 13, 11th month.</ref>
Notable members of the clan include {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}}, who contributed to drafting the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, and {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}}, a poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.<ref>{{Sfn|国史大辞典編集委員会|1986|p=|loc=§. 下毛野氏}}</ref> Over time, the clan's influence waned, and they served as middle- and low-ranking court officials.<ref>{{Sfn|kotobank-下毛野氏|loc=世界大百科事典}}</ref>
== Notable Members == * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}} - Compiler of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} - Deputy general in the conquest of the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}} - Poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="注"}}
'''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="原"}}
'''Citations''' {{reflist}}

The unique "Shimotsuke-type Kofun," characterized by stone chambers in the anterior part and mounds on elevated platforms, are believed to be the tombs of the Shimotsukeno clan.<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref> Nearby, the ruins of the {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}—the clan's temple—further demonstrate their historical significance.<ref>See {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji Ruins|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}.</ref>
== History ==
In {{ill|684|ja|天武天皇13年}}, Emperor Tenmu conferred the title "Ason" on the Shimotsukeno clan as part of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Nobility|ja|八色の姓}}.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Tenmu, year 13, 11th month.</ref>
Notable members of the clan include {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}}, who contributed to drafting the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, and {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}}, a poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.<ref>{{Sfn|国史大辞典編集委員会|1986|p=|loc=§. 下毛野氏}}</ref> Over time, the clan's influence waned, and they served as middle- and low-ranking court officials.<ref>{{Sfn|kotobank-下毛野氏|loc=世界大百科事典}}</ref>
== Notable Members == * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}} - Compiler of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} - Deputy general in the conquest of the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}} - Poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="注"}}
'''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="原"}}
'''Citations''' {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}} * {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} * {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}

revision.text:
'''Shimotsukeno clan''' (下毛野氏)
The '''Shimotsukeno clan''' (しもつけのうじ, しもつけぬうじ<ref group="注">From the Edo period until recently, "Shimotsukeno" was also referred to as "Shimotsukenu." Today, "Shimotsukeno" is considered the correct pronunciation (see {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野#「毛野」の由来と読み}}).</ref>) is a [[Japanese clan]] that derives its name from the region of Shimotsukeno.
This [[imperial clan]] (皇別氏族) claimed descent from {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, a [[prince]] of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. The clan initially used the title "Shimotsukeno no Kimi" (下毛野君) or "Shimotsukeno no Kō" (下毛野公), later adopting the title "Shimotsukeno no Ason" (下毛野朝臣).
== Overview ==
After the [[Taika Reforms]], the Shimotsukeno clan became a mid-tier noble family. By the [[10th century]], they were active as low-ranking military officers within the [[imperial court]]. The clan is mentioned in early chronicles as serving the court, but their activities before the Taika Reforms remain unclear. As their name implies, the clan originated in the Shimotsukeno region (modern-day southwestern [[Tochigi Prefecture]]), though historical evidence is sparse.
The name "{{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}}" refers to an ancient region encompassing modern-day [[Gunma Prefecture]] and southwestern Tochigi Prefecture. The area closer to the capital was called "{{ill|Kamitsukeno|ja|上毛野}}" (later {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}), while the more distant part was termed "{{ill|Shimotsukeno|ja|下毛野}}" (later {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}).
== Origins ==
=== Historical Records ===
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} ordered his son {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}} to govern the eastern provinces. It also identifies Toyokiiribiko as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and Shimotsukeno clan.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Sujin, year 48.</ref> This lineage is similarly recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.<ref group="原">Kojiki, Emperor Sujin's record.</ref>
The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} of {{ill|815|ja|弘仁6年}} states that the Shimotsukeno no Ason descended from Toyokiiribiko.<ref group="原">Shinsen Shōjiroku, Sakyo Imperial Lineage, Shimotsukeno no Ason.</ref> Other clans claiming descent from Toyokiiribiko include {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}, {{ill|Ōno clan|ja|大野氏}}, and {{ill|Kuramochi clan|ja|車持氏}}, collectively known as the {{ill|Tōgoku Six Clan Ason|ja|東国六腹朝臣}}.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Enryaku 10, 4th month.</ref>
The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that in {{ill|707|ja|慶雲4年}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} changed the clan name to "Shimotsukeno Kawachi no Ason," indicating a connection to the Kawachi District.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Keiun 4, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
=== Archaeological Evidence ===
[[File:Azuma-kofun zenkei.JPG|thumb|210px|right|{{ill|Azuma Kofun|ja|吾妻古墳}} in Tochigi Prefecture.]] Numerous {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} burial mounds were constructed in the Keno region during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. In the Shimotsukeno area, the late 5th and early 6th centuries saw the construction of large kofun such as {{ill|Marishitenzuka Kofun|ja|摩利支天塚古墳}} (121 meters) and {{ill|Biwasuka Kofun|ja|琵琶塚古墳}} (123 meters).<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref>
The unique "Shimotsuke-type Kofun," characterized by stone chambers in the anterior part and mounds on elevated platforms, are believed to be the tombs of the Shimotsukeno clan.<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref> Nearby, the ruins of the {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}—the clan's temple—further demonstrate their historical significance.<ref>See {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji Ruins|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}.</ref>
== History ==
In {{ill|684|ja|天武天皇13年}}, Emperor Tenmu conferred the title "Ason" on the Shimotsukeno clan as part of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Nobility|ja|八色の姓}}.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Tenmu, year 13, 11th month.</ref>
Notable members of the clan include {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}}, who contributed to drafting the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, and {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}}, a poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.<ref>{{Sfn|国史大辞典編集委員会|1986|p=|loc=§. 下毛野氏}}</ref> Over time, the clan's influence waned, and they served as middle- and low-ranking court officials.<ref>{{Sfn|kotobank-下毛野氏|loc=世界大百科事典}}</ref>
== Notable Members == * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}} - Compiler of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} - Deputy general in the conquest of the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}} - Poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="注"}}
'''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="原"}}
'''Citations''' {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}} * {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} * {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}

The '''Shimotsukeno clan''' (しもつけのうじ, しもつけぬうじ<ref group="注">From the Edo period until recently, "Shimotsukeno" was also referred to as "Shimotsukenu." Today, "Shimotsukeno" is considered the correct pronunciation (see {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野#「毛野」の由来と読み}}).</ref>) is a [[Japanese clan]] that derives its name from the region of Shimotsukeno.
This [[imperial clan]] (皇別氏族) claimed descent from {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, a [[prince]] of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. The clan initially used the title "Shimotsukeno no Kimi" (下毛野君) or "Shimotsukeno no Kō" (下毛野公), later adopting the title "Shimotsukeno no Ason" (下毛野朝臣).
== Overview ==
After the [[Taika Reforms]], the Shimotsukeno clan became a mid-tier noble family. By the [[10th century]], they were active as low-ranking military officers within the [[imperial court]]. The clan is mentioned in early chronicles as serving the court, but their activities before the Taika Reforms remain unclear. As their name implies, the clan originated in the Shimotsukeno region (modern-day southwestern [[Tochigi Prefecture]]), though historical evidence is sparse.
The name "{{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}}" refers to an ancient region encompassing modern-day [[Gunma Prefecture]] and southwestern Tochigi Prefecture. The area closer to the capital was called "{{ill|Kamitsukeno|ja|上毛野}}" (later {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}), while the more distant part was termed "{{ill|Shimotsukeno|ja|下毛野}}" (later {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}).
== Origins ==
=== Historical Records ===
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} ordered his son {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}} to govern the eastern provinces. It also identifies Toyokiiribiko as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and Shimotsukeno clan.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Sujin, year 48.</ref> This lineage is similarly recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.<ref group="原">Kojiki, Emperor Sujin's record.</ref>
The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} of {{ill|815|ja|弘仁6年}} states that the Shimotsukeno no Ason descended from Toyokiiribiko.<ref group="原">Shinsen Shōjiroku, Sakyo Imperial Lineage, Shimotsukeno no Ason.</ref> Other clans claiming descent from Toyokiiribiko include {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}, {{ill|Ōno clan|ja|大野氏}}, and {{ill|Kuramochi clan|ja|車持氏}}, collectively known as the {{ill|Tōgoku Six Clan Ason|ja|東国六腹朝臣}}.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Enryaku 10, 4th month.</ref>
The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that in {{ill|707|ja|慶雲4年}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} changed the clan name to "Shimotsukeno Kawachi no Ason," indicating a connection to the Kawachi District.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Keiun 4, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
=== Archaeological Evidence ===
[[File:Azuma-kofun zenkei.JPG|thumb|210px|right|{{ill|Azuma Kofun|ja|吾妻古墳}} in Tochigi Prefecture.]] Numerous {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} burial mounds were constructed in the Keno region during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. In the Shimotsukeno area, the late 5th and early 6th centuries saw the construction of large kofun such as {{ill|Marishitenzuka Kofun|ja|摩利支天塚古墳}} (121 meters) and {{ill|Biwasuka Kofun|ja|琵琶塚古墳}} (123 meters).<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref>
The unique "Shimotsuke-type Kofun," characterized by stone chambers in the anterior part and mounds on elevated platforms, are believed to be the tombs of the Shimotsukeno clan.<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref> Nearby, the ruins of the {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}—the clan's temple—further demonstrate their historical significance.<ref>See {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji Ruins|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}.</ref>
== History ==
In {{ill|684|ja|天武天皇13年}}, Emperor Tenmu conferred the title "Ason" on the Shimotsukeno clan as part of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Nobility|ja|八色の姓}}.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Tenmu, year 13, 11th month.</ref>
Notable members of the clan include {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}}, who contributed to drafting the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, and {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}}, a poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.<ref>{{Sfn|国史大辞典編集委員会|1986|p=|loc=§. 下毛野氏}}</ref> Over time, the clan's influence waned, and they served as middle- and low-ranking court officials.<ref>{{Sfn|kotobank-下毛野氏|loc=世界大百科事典}}</ref>
== Notable Members == * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}} - Compiler of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} - Deputy general in the conquest of the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}} - Poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="注"}}
'''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="原"}}
'''Citations''' {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}} * {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} * {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}

The unique "Shimotsuke-type Kofun," characterized by stone chambers in the anterior part and mounds on elevated platforms, are believed to be the tombs of the Shimotsukeno clan.<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref> Nearby, the ruins of the {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}—the clan's temple—further demonstrate their historical significance.<ref>See {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji Ruins|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}.</ref>
== History ==
In {{ill|684|ja|天武天皇13年}}, Emperor Tenmu conferred the title "Ason" on the Shimotsukeno clan as part of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Nobility|ja|八色の姓}}.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Tenmu, year 13, 11th month.</ref>
Notable members of the clan include {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}}, who contributed to drafting the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, and {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}}, a poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.<ref>{{Sfn|国史大辞典編集委員会|1986|p=|loc=§. 下毛野氏}}</ref> Over time, the clan's influence waned, and they served as middle- and low-ranking court officials.<ref>{{Sfn|kotobank-下毛野氏|loc=世界大百科事典}}</ref>
== Notable Members == * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}} - Compiler of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} - Deputy general in the conquest of the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}} - Poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.

'''Shimotsukeno clan''' (下毛野氏)
The '''Shimotsukeno clan''' (しもつけのうじ, しもつけぬうじ<ref group="注">From the Edo period until recently, "Shimotsukeno" was also referred to as "Shimotsukenu." Today, "Shimotsukeno" is considered the correct pronunciation (see {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野#「毛野」の由来と読み}}).</ref>) is a [[Japanese clan]] that derives its name from the region of Shimotsukeno.
This [[imperial clan]] (皇別氏族) claimed descent from {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, a [[prince]] of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. The clan initially used the title "Shimotsukeno no Kimi" (下毛野君) or "Shimotsukeno no Kō" (下毛野公), later adopting the title "Shimotsukeno no Ason" (下毛野朝臣).
== Overview ==
After the [[Taika Reforms]], the Shimotsukeno clan became a mid-tier noble family. By the [[10th century]], they were active as low-ranking military officers within the [[imperial court]]. The clan is mentioned in early chronicles as serving the court, but their activities before the Taika Reforms remain unclear. As their name implies, the clan originated in the Shimotsukeno region (modern-day southwestern [[Tochigi Prefecture]]), though historical evidence is sparse.
The name "{{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}}" refers to an ancient region encompassing modern-day [[Gunma Prefecture]] and southwestern Tochigi Prefecture. The area closer to the capital was called "{{ill|Kamitsukeno|ja|上毛野}}" (later {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}), while the more distant part was termed "{{ill|Shimotsukeno|ja|下毛野}}" (later {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}).
== Origins ==
=== Historical Records ===
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} ordered his son {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}} to govern the eastern provinces. It also identifies Toyokiiribiko as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and Shimotsukeno clan.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Sujin, year 48.</ref> This lineage is similarly recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.<ref group="原">Kojiki, Emperor Sujin's record.</ref>
The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} of {{ill|815|ja|弘仁6年}} states that the Shimotsukeno no Ason descended from Toyokiiribiko.<ref group="原">Shinsen Shōjiroku, Sakyo Imperial Lineage, Shimotsukeno no Ason.</ref> Other clans claiming descent from Toyokiiribiko include {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}, {{ill|Ōno clan|ja|大野氏}}, and {{ill|Kuramochi clan|ja|車持氏}}, collectively known as the {{ill|Tōgoku Six Clan Ason|ja|東国六腹朝臣}}.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Enryaku 10, 4th month.</ref>
The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that in {{ill|707|ja|慶雲4年}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} changed the clan name to "Shimotsukeno Kawachi no Ason," indicating a connection to the Kawachi District.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Keiun 4, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
=== Archaeological Evidence ===
[[File:Azuma-kofun zenkei.JPG|thumb|210px|right|{{ill|Azuma Kofun|ja|吾妻古墳}} in Tochigi Prefecture.]] Numerous {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} burial mounds were constructed in the Keno region during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. In the Shimotsukeno area, the late 5th and early 6th centuries saw the construction of large kofun such as {{ill|Marishitenzuka Kofun|ja|摩利支天塚古墳}} (121 meters) and {{ill|Biwasuka Kofun|ja|琵琶塚古墳}} (123 meters).<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref>
The unique "Shimotsuke-type Kofun," characterized by stone chambers in the anterior part and mounds on elevated platforms, are believed to be the tombs of the Shimotsukeno clan.<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref> Nearby, the ruins of the {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}—the clan's temple—further demonstrate their historical significance.<ref>See {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji Ruins|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}.</ref>
== History ==
In {{ill|684|ja|天武天皇13年}}, Emperor Tenmu conferred the title "Ason" on the Shimotsukeno clan as part of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Nobility|ja|八色の姓}}.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Tenmu, year 13, 11th month.</ref>
Notable members of the clan include {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}}, who contributed to drafting the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, and {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}}, a poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.<ref>{{Sfn|国史大辞典編集委員会|1986|p=|loc=§. 下毛野氏}}</ref> Over time, the clan's influence waned, and they served as middle- and low-ranking court officials.<ref>{{Sfn|kotobank-下毛野氏|loc=世界大百科事典}}</ref>
== Notable Members == * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}} - Compiler of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} - Deputy general in the conquest of the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}} - Poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="注"}}

'''Citations''' {{reflist}}

[[Category:bump]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Shimotsuke clan]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[ja:下毛野氏]]

'''Shimotsukeno clan''' (下毛野氏)
The '''Shimotsukeno clan''' (しもつけのうじ, しもつけぬうじ<ref group="注">From the Edo period until recently, "Shimotsukeno" was also referred to as "Shimotsukenu." Today, "Shimotsukeno" is considered the correct pronunciation (see {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野#「毛野」の由来と読み}}).</ref>) is a [[Japanese clan]] that derives its name from the region of Shimotsukeno.
This [[imperial clan]] (皇別氏族) claimed descent from {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, a [[prince]] of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. The clan initially used the title "Shimotsukeno no Kimi" (下毛野君) or "Shimotsukeno no Kō" (下毛野公), later adopting the title "Shimotsukeno no Ason" (下毛野朝臣).
== Overview ==
After the [[Taika Reforms]], the Shimotsukeno clan became a mid-tier noble family. By the [[10th century]], they were active as low-ranking military officers within the [[imperial court]]. The clan is mentioned in early chronicles as serving the court, but their activities before the Taika Reforms remain unclear. As their name implies, the clan originated in the Shimotsukeno region (modern-day southwestern [[Tochigi Prefecture]]), though historical evidence is sparse.
The name "{{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}}" refers to an ancient region encompassing modern-day [[Gunma Prefecture]] and southwestern Tochigi Prefecture. The area closer to the capital was called "{{ill|Kamitsukeno|ja|上毛野}}" (later {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}), while the more distant part was termed "{{ill|Shimotsukeno|ja|下毛野}}" (later {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}).
== Origins ==
=== Historical Records ===
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} ordered his son {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}} to govern the eastern provinces. It also identifies Toyokiiribiko as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and Shimotsukeno clan.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Sujin, year 48.</ref> This lineage is similarly recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.<ref group="原">Kojiki, Emperor Sujin's record.</ref>
The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} of {{ill|815|ja|弘仁6年}} states that the Shimotsukeno no Ason descended from Toyokiiribiko.<ref group="原">Shinsen Shōjiroku, Sakyo Imperial Lineage, Shimotsukeno no Ason.</ref> Other clans claiming descent from Toyokiiribiko include {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}, {{ill|Ōno clan|ja|大野氏}}, and {{ill|Kuramochi clan|ja|車持氏}}, collectively known as the {{ill|Tōgoku Six Clan Ason|ja|東国六腹朝臣}}.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Enryaku 10, 4th month.</ref>
The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that in {{ill|707|ja|慶雲4年}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} changed the clan name to "Shimotsukeno Kawachi no Ason," indicating a connection to the Kawachi District.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Keiun 4, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
=== Archaeological Evidence ===
[[File:Azuma-kofun zenkei.JPG|thumb|210px|right|{{ill|Azuma Kofun|ja|吾妻古墳}} in Tochigi Prefecture.]] Numerous {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} burial mounds were constructed in the Keno region during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. In the Shimotsukeno area, the late 5th and early 6th centuries saw the construction of large kofun such as {{ill|Marishitenzuka Kofun|ja|摩利支天塚古墳}} (121 meters) and {{ill|Biwasuka Kofun|ja|琵琶塚古墳}} (123 meters).<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref>
The unique "Shimotsuke-type Kofun," characterized by stone chambers in the anterior part and mounds on elevated platforms, are believed to be the tombs of the Shimotsukeno clan.<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref> Nearby, the ruins of the {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}—the clan's temple—further demonstrate their historical significance.<ref>See {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji Ruins|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}.</ref>
== History ==
In {{ill|684|ja|天武天皇13年}}, Emperor Tenmu conferred the title "Ason" on the Shimotsukeno clan as part of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Nobility|ja|八色の姓}}.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Tenmu, year 13, 11th month.</ref>
Notable members of the clan include {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}}, who contributed to drafting the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, and {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}}, a poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.<ref>{{Sfn|国史大辞典編集委員会|1986|p=|loc=§. 下毛野氏}}</ref> Over time, the clan's influence waned, and they served as middle- and low-ranking court officials.<ref>{{Sfn|kotobank-下毛野氏|loc=世界大百科事典}}</ref>
== Notable Members == * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}} - Compiler of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} - Deputy general in the conquest of the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}} - Poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.

The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} ordered his son {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}} to govern the eastern provinces. It also identifies Toyokiiribiko as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and Shimotsukeno clan.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Sujin, year 48.</ref> This lineage is similarly recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.<ref group="原">Kojiki, Emperor Sujin's record.</ref>
The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} of {{ill|815|ja|弘仁6年}} states that the Shimotsukeno no Ason descended from Toyokiiribiko.<ref group="原">Shinsen Shōjiroku, Sakyo Imperial Lineage, Shimotsukeno no Ason.</ref> Other clans claiming descent from Toyokiiribiko include {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}, {{ill|Ōno clan|ja|大野氏}}, and {{ill|Kuramochi clan|ja|車持氏}}, collectively known as the {{ill|Tōgoku Six Clan Ason|ja|東国六腹朝臣}}.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Enryaku 10, 4th month.</ref>
The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that in {{ill|707|ja|慶雲4年}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} changed the clan name to "Shimotsukeno Kawachi no Ason," indicating a connection to the Kawachi District.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Keiun 4, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
=== Archaeological Evidence ===
[[File:Azuma-kofun zenkei.JPG|thumb|210px|right|{{ill|Azuma Kofun|ja|吾妻古墳}} in Tochigi Prefecture.]] Numerous {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} burial mounds were constructed in the Keno region during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. In the Shimotsukeno area, the late 5th and early 6th centuries saw the construction of large kofun such as {{ill|Marishitenzuka Kofun|ja|摩利支天塚古墳}} (121 meters) and {{ill|Biwasuka Kofun|ja|琵琶塚古墳}} (123 meters).<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref>
The unique "Shimotsuke-type Kofun," characterized by stone chambers in the anterior part and mounds on elevated platforms, are believed to be the tombs of the Shimotsukeno clan.<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref> Nearby, the ruins of the {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}—the clan's temple—further demonstrate their historical significance.<ref>See {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji Ruins|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}.</ref>
== History ==
In {{ill|684|ja|天武天皇13年}}, Emperor Tenmu conferred the title "Ason" on the Shimotsukeno clan as part of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Nobility|ja|八色の姓}}.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Tenmu, year 13, 11th month.</ref>
Notable members of the clan include {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}}, who contributed to drafting the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, and {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}}, a poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.<ref>{{Sfn|国史大辞典編集委員会|1986|p=|loc=§. 下毛野氏}}</ref> Over time, the clan's influence waned, and they served as middle- and low-ranking court officials.<ref>{{Sfn|kotobank-下毛野氏|loc=世界大百科事典}}</ref>
== Notable Members == * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}} - Compiler of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} - Deputy general in the conquest of the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}} - Poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.

[[Category:bump]] [[Category:Shimotsuke clan]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[ja:下毛野氏]]

'''Shimotsukeno clan''' (下毛野氏)
The '''Shimotsukeno clan''' (しもつけのうじ, しもつけぬうじ<ref group="注">From the Edo period until recently, "Shimotsukeno" was also referred to as "Shimotsukenu." Today, "Shimotsukeno" is considered the correct pronunciation (see {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野#「毛野」の由来と読み}}).</ref>) is a [[Japanese clan]] that derives its name from the region of Shimotsukeno.
This [[imperial clan]] (皇別氏族) claimed descent from {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, a [[prince]] of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. The clan initially used the title "Shimotsukeno no Kimi" (下毛野君) or "Shimotsukeno no Kō" (下毛野公), later adopting the title "Shimotsukeno no Ason" (下毛野朝臣).
== Overview ==
After the [[Taika Reforms]], the Shimotsukeno clan became a mid-tier noble family. By the [[10th century]], they were active as low-ranking military officers within the [[imperial court]]. The clan is mentioned in early chronicles as serving the court, but their activities before the Taika Reforms remain unclear. As their name implies, the clan originated in the Shimotsukeno region (modern-day southwestern [[Tochigi Prefecture]]), though historical evidence is sparse.
The name "{{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}}" refers to an ancient region encompassing modern-day [[Gunma Prefecture]] and southwestern Tochigi Prefecture. The area closer to the capital was called "{{ill|Kamitsukeno|ja|上毛野}}" (later {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}), while the more distant part was termed "{{ill|Shimotsukeno|ja|下毛野}}" (later {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}).
== Origins ==
=== Historical Records ===
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} ordered his son {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}} to govern the eastern provinces. It also identifies Toyokiiribiko as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and Shimotsukeno clan.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Sujin, year 48.</ref> This lineage is similarly recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.<ref group="原">Kojiki, Emperor Sujin's record.</ref>
The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} of {{ill|815|ja|弘仁6年}} states that the Shimotsukeno no Ason descended from Toyokiiribiko.<ref group="原">Shinsen Shōjiroku, Sakyo Imperial Lineage, Shimotsukeno no Ason.</ref> Other clans claiming descent from Toyokiiribiko include {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}, {{ill|Ōno clan|ja|大野氏}}, and {{ill|Kuramochi clan|ja|車持氏}}, collectively known as the {{ill|Tōgoku Six Clan Ason|ja|東国六腹朝臣}}.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Enryaku 10, 4th month.</ref>
The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that in {{ill|707|ja|慶雲4年}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} changed the clan name to "Shimotsukeno Kawachi no Ason," indicating a connection to the Kawachi District.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Keiun 4, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
=== Archaeological Evidence ===
[[File:Azuma-kofun zenkei.JPG|thumb|210px|right|{{ill|Azuma Kofun|ja|吾妻古墳}} in Tochigi Prefecture.]] Numerous {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} burial mounds were constructed in the Keno region during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. In the Shimotsukeno area, the late 5th and early 6th centuries saw the construction of large kofun such as {{ill|Marishitenzuka Kofun|ja|摩利支天塚古墳}} (121 meters) and {{ill|Biwasuka Kofun|ja|琵琶塚古墳}} (123 meters).<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref>
The unique "Shimotsuke-type Kofun," characterized by stone chambers in the anterior part and mounds on elevated platforms, are believed to be the tombs of the Shimotsukeno clan.<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref> Nearby, the ruins of the {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}—the clan's temple—further demonstrate their historical significance.<ref>See {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji Ruins|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}.</ref>
== History ==
In {{ill|684|ja|天武天皇13年}}, Emperor Tenmu conferred the title "Ason" on the Shimotsukeno clan as part of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Nobility|ja|八色の姓}}.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Tenmu, year 13, 11th month.</ref>
Notable members of the clan include {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}}, who contributed to drafting the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, and {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}}, a poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.<ref>{{Sfn|国史大辞典編集委員会|1986|p=|loc=§. 下毛野氏}}</ref> Over time, the clan's influence waned, and they served as middle- and low-ranking court officials.<ref>{{Sfn|kotobank-下毛野氏|loc=世界大百科事典}}</ref>
== Notable Members == * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}} - Compiler of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} - Deputy general in the conquest of the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}} - Poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.

== History ==
In {{ill|684|ja|天武天皇13年}}, Emperor Tenmu conferred the title "Ason" on the Shimotsukeno clan as part of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Nobility|ja|八色の姓}}.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Tenmu, year 13, 11th month.</ref>
Notable members of the clan include {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}}, who contributed to drafting the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, and {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}}, a poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.<ref>{{Sfn|国史大辞典編集委員会|1986|p=|loc=§. 下毛野氏}}</ref> Over time, the clan's influence waned, and they served as middle- and low-ranking court officials.<ref>{{Sfn|kotobank-下毛野氏|loc=世界大百科事典}}</ref>
== Notable Members == * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}} - Compiler of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} - Deputy general in the conquest of the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}} - Poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="注"}}
'''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="原"}}
'''Citations''' {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}} * {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} * {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}

revision.text:
'''Shimotsukeno clan''' (下毛野氏)
The '''Shimotsukeno clan''' (しもつけのうじ, しもつけぬうじ<ref group="注">From the Edo period until recently, "Shimotsukeno" was also referred to as "Shimotsukenu." Today, "Shimotsukeno" is considered the correct pronunciation (see {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野#「毛野」の由来と読み}}).</ref>) is a [[Japanese clan]] that derives its name from the region of Shimotsukeno.
This [[imperial clan]] (皇別氏族) claimed descent from {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, a [[prince]] of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. The clan initially used the title "Shimotsukeno no Kimi" (下毛野君) or "Shimotsukeno no Kō" (下毛野公), later adopting the title "Shimotsukeno no Ason" (下毛野朝臣).
== Overview ==
After the [[Taika Reforms]], the Shimotsukeno clan became a mid-tier noble family. By the [[10th century]], they were active as low-ranking military officers within the [[imperial court]]. The clan is mentioned in early chronicles as serving the court, but their activities before the Taika Reforms remain unclear. As their name implies, the clan originated in the Shimotsukeno region (modern-day southwestern [[Tochigi Prefecture]]), though historical evidence is sparse.
The name "{{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}}" refers to an ancient region encompassing modern-day [[Gunma Prefecture]] and southwestern Tochigi Prefecture. The area closer to the capital was called "{{ill|Kamitsukeno|ja|上毛野}}" (later {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}), while the more distant part was termed "{{ill|Shimotsukeno|ja|下毛野}}" (later {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}).
== Origins ==
=== Historical Records ===
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} ordered his son {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}} to govern the eastern provinces. It also identifies Toyokiiribiko as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and Shimotsukeno clan.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Sujin, year 48.</ref> This lineage is similarly recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.<ref group="原">Kojiki, Emperor Sujin's record.</ref>
The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} of {{ill|815|ja|弘仁6年}} states that the Shimotsukeno no Ason descended from Toyokiiribiko.<ref group="原">Shinsen Shōjiroku, Sakyo Imperial Lineage, Shimotsukeno no Ason.</ref> Other clans claiming descent from Toyokiiribiko include {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}, {{ill|Ōno clan|ja|大野氏}}, and {{ill|Kuramochi clan|ja|車持氏}}, collectively known as the {{ill|Tōgoku Six Clan Ason|ja|東国六腹朝臣}}.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Enryaku 10, 4th month.</ref>
The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that in {{ill|707|ja|慶雲4年}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} changed the clan name to "Shimotsukeno Kawachi no Ason," indicating a connection to the Kawachi District.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Keiun 4, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
=== Archaeological Evidence ===
[[File:Azuma-kofun zenkei.JPG|thumb|210px|right|{{ill|Azuma Kofun|ja|吾妻古墳}} in Tochigi Prefecture.]] Numerous {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} burial mounds were constructed in the Keno region during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. In the Shimotsukeno area, the late 5th and early 6th centuries saw the construction of large kofun such as {{ill|Marishitenzuka Kofun|ja|摩利支天塚古墳}} (121 meters) and {{ill|Biwasuka Kofun|ja|琵琶塚古墳}} (123 meters).<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref>
The unique "Shimotsuke-type Kofun," characterized by stone chambers in the anterior part and mounds on elevated platforms, are believed to be the tombs of the Shimotsukeno clan.<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref> Nearby, the ruins of the {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}—the clan's temple—further demonstrate their historical significance.<ref>See {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji Ruins|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}.</ref>
== History ==
In {{ill|684|ja|天武天皇13年}}, Emperor Tenmu conferred the title "Ason" on the Shimotsukeno clan as part of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Nobility|ja|八色の姓}}.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Tenmu, year 13, 11th month.</ref>
Notable members of the clan include {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}}, who contributed to drafting the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, and {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}}, a poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.<ref>{{Sfn|国史大辞典編集委員会|1986|p=|loc=§. 下毛野氏}}</ref> Over time, the clan's influence waned, and they served as middle- and low-ranking court officials.<ref>{{Sfn|kotobank-下毛野氏|loc=世界大百科事典}}</ref>
== Notable Members == * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}} - Compiler of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} - Deputy general in the conquest of the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}} - Poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="注"}}
'''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="原"}}
'''Citations''' {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}} * {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} * {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}

revision.text:
'''Shimotsukeno clan''' (下毛野氏)
The '''Shimotsukeno clan''' (しもつけのうじ, しもつけぬうじ<ref group="注">From the Edo period until recently, "Shimotsukeno" was also referred to as "Shimotsukenu." Today, "Shimotsukeno" is considered the correct pronunciation (see {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野#「毛野」の由来と読み}}).</ref>) is a [[Japanese clan]] that derives its name from the region of Shimotsukeno.
This [[imperial clan]] (皇別氏族) claimed descent from {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, a [[prince]] of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. The clan initially used the title "Shimotsukeno no Kimi" (下毛野君) or "Shimotsukeno no Kō" (下毛野公), later adopting the title "Shimotsukeno no Ason" (下毛野朝臣).
== Overview ==
After the [[Taika Reforms]], the Shimotsukeno clan became a mid-tier noble family. By the [[10th century]], they were active as low-ranking military officers within the [[imperial court]]. The clan is mentioned in early chronicles as serving the court, but their activities before the Taika Reforms remain unclear. As their name implies, the clan originated in the Shimotsukeno region (modern-day southwestern [[Tochigi Prefecture]]), though historical evidence is sparse.
The name "{{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}}" refers to an ancient region encompassing modern-day [[Gunma Prefecture]] and southwestern Tochigi Prefecture. The area closer to the capital was called "{{ill|Kamitsukeno|ja|上毛野}}" (later {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}), while the more distant part was termed "{{ill|Shimotsukeno|ja|下毛野}}" (later {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}).
== Origins ==
=== Historical Records ===
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} ordered his son {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}} to govern the eastern provinces. It also identifies Toyokiiribiko as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and Shimotsukeno clan.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Sujin, year 48.</ref> This lineage is similarly recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.<ref group="原">Kojiki, Emperor Sujin's record.</ref>
The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} of {{ill|815|ja|弘仁6年}} states that the Shimotsukeno no Ason descended from Toyokiiribiko.<ref group="原">Shinsen Shōjiroku, Sakyo Imperial Lineage, Shimotsukeno no Ason.</ref> Other clans claiming descent from Toyokiiribiko include {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}, {{ill|Ōno clan|ja|大野氏}}, and {{ill|Kuramochi clan|ja|車持氏}}, collectively known as the {{ill|Tōgoku Six Clan Ason|ja|東国六腹朝臣}}.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Enryaku 10, 4th month.</ref>
The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that in {{ill|707|ja|慶雲4年}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} changed the clan name to "Shimotsukeno Kawachi no Ason," indicating a connection to the Kawachi District.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Keiun 4, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
=== Archaeological Evidence ===
[[File:Azuma-kofun zenkei.JPG|thumb|210px|right|{{ill|Azuma Kofun|ja|吾妻古墳}} in Tochigi Prefecture.]] Numerous {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} burial mounds were constructed in the Keno region during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. In the Shimotsukeno area, the late 5th and early 6th centuries saw the construction of large kofun such as {{ill|Marishitenzuka Kofun|ja|摩利支天塚古墳}} (121 meters) and {{ill|Biwasuka Kofun|ja|琵琶塚古墳}} (123 meters).<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref>
The unique "Shimotsuke-type Kofun," characterized by stone chambers in the anterior part and mounds on elevated platforms, are believed to be the tombs of the Shimotsukeno clan.<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref> Nearby, the ruins of the {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}—the clan's temple—further demonstrate their historical significance.<ref>See {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji Ruins|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}.</ref>
== History ==
In {{ill|684|ja|天武天皇13年}}, Emperor Tenmu conferred the title "Ason" on the Shimotsukeno clan as part of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Nobility|ja|八色の姓}}.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Tenmu, year 13, 11th month.</ref>
Notable members of the clan include {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}}, who contributed to drafting the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, and {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}}, a poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.<ref>{{Sfn|国史大辞典編集委員会|1986|p=|loc=§. 下毛野氏}}</ref> Over time, the clan's influence waned, and they served as middle- and low-ranking court officials.<ref>{{Sfn|kotobank-下毛野氏|loc=世界大百科事典}}</ref>
== Notable Members == * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}} - Compiler of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} - Deputy general in the conquest of the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}} - Poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="注"}}
'''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="原"}}
'''Citations''' {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}} * {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} * {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Pages needing attribution]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Shimotsukeno clan''' (下毛野氏)
The '''Shimotsukeno clan''' (しもつけのうじ, しもつけぬうじ<ref group="注">From the Edo period until recently, "Shimotsukeno" was also referred to as "Shimotsukenu." Today, "Shimotsukeno" is considered the correct pronunciation (see {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野#「毛野」の由来と読み}}).</ref>) is a [[Japanese clan]] that derives its name from the region of Shimotsukeno.
This [[imperial clan]] (皇別氏族) claimed descent from {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, a [[prince]] of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. The clan initially used the title "Shimotsukeno no Kimi" (下毛野君) or "Shimotsukeno no Kō" (下毛野公), later adopting the title "Shimotsukeno no Ason" (下毛野朝臣).
== Overview ==
After the [[Taika Reforms]], the Shimotsukeno clan became a mid-tier noble family. By the [[10th century]], they were active as low-ranking military officers within the [[imperial court]]. The clan is mentioned in early chronicles as serving the court, but their activities before the Taika Reforms remain unclear. As their name implies, the clan originated in the Shimotsukeno region (modern-day southwestern [[Tochigi Prefecture]]), though historical evidence is sparse.
The name "{{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}}" refers to an ancient region encompassing modern-day [[Gunma Prefecture]] and southwestern Tochigi Prefecture. The area closer to the capital was called "{{ill|Kamitsukeno|ja|上毛野}}" (later {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}), while the more distant part was termed "{{ill|Shimotsukeno|ja|下毛野}}" (later {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}).
== Origins ==
=== Historical Records ===
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} ordered his son {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}} to govern the eastern provinces. It also identifies Toyokiiribiko as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and Shimotsukeno clan.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Sujin, year 48.</ref> This lineage is similarly recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.<ref group="原">Kojiki, Emperor Sujin's record.</ref>
The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} of {{ill|815|ja|弘仁6年}} states that the Shimotsukeno no Ason descended from Toyokiiribiko.<ref group="原">Shinsen Shōjiroku, Sakyo Imperial Lineage, Shimotsukeno no Ason.</ref> Other clans claiming descent from Toyokiiribiko include {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}, {{ill|Ōno clan|ja|大野氏}}, and {{ill|Kuramochi clan|ja|車持氏}}, collectively known as the {{ill|Tōgoku Six Clan Ason|ja|東国六腹朝臣}}.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Enryaku 10, 4th month.</ref>
The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that in {{ill|707|ja|慶雲4年}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} changed the clan name to "Shimotsukeno Kawachi no Ason," indicating a connection to the Kawachi District.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Keiun 4, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
=== Archaeological Evidence ===
[[File:Azuma-kofun zenkei.JPG|thumb|210px|right|{{ill|Azuma Kofun|ja|吾妻古墳}} in Tochigi Prefecture.]] Numerous {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} burial mounds were constructed in the Keno region during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. In the Shimotsukeno area, the late 5th and early 6th centuries saw the construction of large kofun such as {{ill|Marishitenzuka Kofun|ja|摩利支天塚古墳}} (121 meters) and {{ill|Biwasuka Kofun|ja|琵琶塚古墳}} (123 meters).<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref>
The unique "Shimotsuke-type Kofun," characterized by stone chambers in the anterior part and mounds on elevated platforms, are believed to be the tombs of the Shimotsukeno clan.<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref> Nearby, the ruins of the {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}—the clan's temple—further demonstrate their historical significance.<ref>See {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji Ruins|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}.</ref>
== History ==
In {{ill|684|ja|天武天皇13年}}, Emperor Tenmu conferred the title "Ason" on the Shimotsukeno clan as part of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Nobility|ja|八色の姓}}.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Tenmu, year 13, 11th month.</ref>
Notable members of the clan include {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}}, who contributed to drafting the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, and {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}}, a poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.<ref>{{Sfn|国史大辞典編集委員会|1986|p=|loc=§. 下毛野氏}}</ref> Over time, the clan's influence waned, and they served as middle- and low-ranking court officials.<ref>{{Sfn|kotobank-下毛野氏|loc=世界大百科事典}}</ref>
== Notable Members == * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}} - Compiler of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} - Deputy general in the conquest of the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}} - Poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="注"}}
'''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="原"}}
'''Citations''' {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}} * {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} * {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}

'''Shimotsukeno clan''' (下毛野氏)
The '''Shimotsukeno clan''' (しもつけのうじ, しもつけぬうじ<ref group="注">From the Edo period until recently, "Shimotsukeno" was also referred to as "Shimotsukenu." == Notable Members == * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}} - Compiler of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} - Deputy general in the conquest of the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}} - Poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:PAGENAME}}-qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Shimotsukeno clan''' (下毛野氏)
The '''Shimotsukeno clan''' (しもつけのうじ, しもつけぬうじ<ref group="注">From the Edo period until recently, "Shimotsukeno" was also referred to as "Shimotsukenu." Today, "Shimotsukeno" is considered the correct pronunciation (see {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野#「毛野」の由来と読み}}).</ref>) is a [[Japanese clan]] that derives its name from the region of Shimotsukeno.
This [[imperial clan]] (皇別氏族) claimed descent from {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, a [[prince]] of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. The clan initially used the title "Shimotsukeno no Kimi" (下毛野君) or "Shimotsukeno no Kō" (下毛野公), later adopting the title "Shimotsukeno no Ason" (下毛野朝臣).
== Overview ==
After the [[Taika Reforms]], the Shimotsukeno clan became a mid-tier noble family. By the [[10th century]], they were active as low-ranking military officers within the [[imperial court]]. The clan is mentioned in early chronicles as serving the court, but their activities before the Taika Reforms remain unclear. As their name implies, the clan originated in the Shimotsukeno region (modern-day southwestern [[Tochigi Prefecture]]), though historical evidence is sparse.
The name "{{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}}" refers to an ancient region encompassing modern-day [[Gunma Prefecture]] and southwestern Tochigi Prefecture. The area closer to the capital was called "{{ill|Kamitsukeno|ja|上毛野}}" (later {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}), while the more distant part was termed "{{ill|Shimotsukeno|ja|下毛野}}" (later {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}).
== Origins ==
=== Historical Records ===
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} ordered his son {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}} to govern the eastern provinces. It also identifies Toyokiiribiko as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and Shimotsukeno clan.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Sujin, year 48.</ref> This lineage is similarly recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.<ref group="原">Kojiki, Emperor Sujin's record.</ref>
The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} of {{ill|815|ja|弘仁6年}} states that the Shimotsukeno no Ason descended from Toyokiiribiko.<ref group="原">Shinsen Shōjiroku, Sakyo Imperial Lineage, Shimotsukeno no Ason.</ref> Other clans claiming descent from Toyokiiribiko include {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}, {{ill|Ōno clan|ja|大野氏}}, and {{ill|Kuramochi clan|ja|車持氏}}, collectively known as the {{ill|Tōgoku Six Clan Ason|ja|東国六腹朝臣}}.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Enryaku 10, 4th month.</ref>
The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that in {{ill|707|ja|慶雲4年}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} changed the clan name to "Shimotsukeno Kawachi no Ason," indicating a connection to the Kawachi District.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Keiun 4, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
=== Archaeological Evidence ===
[[File:Azuma-kofun zenkei.JPG|thumb|210px|right|{{ill|Azuma Kofun|ja|吾妻古墳}} in Tochigi Prefecture.]] Numerous {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} burial mounds were constructed in the Keno region during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. In the Shimotsukeno area, the late 5th and early 6th centuries saw the construction of large kofun such as {{ill|Marishitenzuka Kofun|ja|摩利支天塚古墳}} (121 meters) and {{ill|Biwasuka Kofun|ja|琵琶塚古墳}} (123 meters).<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref>
The unique "Shimotsuke-type Kofun," characterized by stone chambers in the anterior part and mounds on elevated platforms, are believed to be the tombs of the Shimotsukeno clan.<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref> Nearby, the ruins of the {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}—the clan's temple—further demonstrate their historical significance.<ref>See {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji Ruins|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}.</ref>
== History ==
In {{ill|684|ja|天武天皇13年}}, Emperor Tenmu conferred the title "Ason" on the Shimotsukeno clan as part of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Nobility|ja|八色の姓}}.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Tenmu, year 13, 11th month.</ref>
Notable members of the clan include {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}}, who contributed to drafting the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, and {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}}, a poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.<ref>{{Sfn|国史大辞典編集委員会|1986|p=|loc=§. 下毛野氏}}</ref> Over time, the clan's influence waned, and they served as middle- and low-ranking court officials.<ref>{{Sfn|kotobank-下毛野氏|loc=世界大百科事典}}</ref>
== Notable Members == * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}} - Compiler of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} - Deputy general in the conquest of the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}} - Poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="注"}}
'''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="原"}}
'''Citations''' {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}} * {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} * {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}

'''Shimotsukeno clan''' (下毛野氏)
The '''Shimotsukeno clan''' (しもつけのうじ, しもつけぬうじ<ref group="注">From the Edo period until recently, "Shimotsukeno" was also referred to as "Shimotsukenu." Today, "Shimotsukeno" is considered the correct pronunciation (see {{ill|Keno|ja|毛野#「毛野」の由来と読み}}).</ref>) is a [[Japanese clan]] that derives its name from the region of Shimotsukeno.
This [[imperial clan]] (皇別氏族) claimed descent from {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, a [[prince]] of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. The clan initially used the title "Shimotsukeno no Kimi" (下毛野君) or "Shimotsukeno no Kō" (下毛野公), later adopting the title "Shimotsukeno no Ason" (下毛野朝臣).
== Overview ==
After the [[Taika Reforms]], the Shimotsukeno clan became a mid-tier noble family. By the [[10th century]], they were active as low-ranking military officers within the [[imperial court]]. The clan is mentioned in early chronicles as serving the court, but their activities before the Taika Reforms remain unclear. As their name implies, the clan originated in the Shimotsukeno region (modern-day southwestern [[Tochigi Prefecture]]), though historical evidence is sparse.
The name "{{ill|Keno|ja|毛野}}" refers to an ancient region encompassing modern-day [[Gunma Prefecture]] and southwestern Tochigi Prefecture. The area closer to the capital was called "{{ill|Kamitsukeno|ja|上毛野}}" (later {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}), while the more distant part was termed "{{ill|Shimotsukeno|ja|下毛野}}" (later {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}).
== Origins ==
=== Historical Records ===
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} ordered his son {{ill|Toyokiiribiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}} to govern the eastern provinces. It also identifies Toyokiiribiko as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and Shimotsukeno clan.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Sujin, year 48.</ref> This lineage is similarly recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.<ref group="原">Kojiki, Emperor Sujin's record.</ref>
The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}} of {{ill|815|ja|弘仁6年}} states that the Shimotsukeno no Ason descended from Toyokiiribiko.<ref group="原">Shinsen Shōjiroku, Sakyo Imperial Lineage, Shimotsukeno no Ason.</ref> Other clans claiming descent from Toyokiiribiko include {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}, {{ill|Ōno clan|ja|大野氏}}, and {{ill|Kuramochi clan|ja|車持氏}}, collectively known as the {{ill|Tōgoku Six Clan Ason|ja|東国六腹朝臣}}.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Enryaku 10, 4th month.</ref>
The {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} records that in {{ill|707|ja|慶雲4年}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} changed the clan name to "Shimotsukeno Kawachi no Ason," indicating a connection to the Kawachi District.<ref group="原">Shoku Nihongi, Keiun 4, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
=== Archaeological Evidence ===
[[File:Azuma-kofun zenkei.JPG|thumb|210px|right|{{ill|Azuma Kofun|ja|吾妻古墳}} in Tochigi Prefecture.]] Numerous {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} burial mounds were constructed in the Keno region during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. In the Shimotsukeno area, the late 5th and early 6th centuries saw the construction of large kofun such as {{ill|Marishitenzuka Kofun|ja|摩利支天塚古墳}} (121 meters) and {{ill|Biwasuka Kofun|ja|琵琶塚古墳}} (123 meters).<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref>
The unique "Shimotsuke-type Kofun," characterized by stone chambers in the anterior part and mounds on elevated platforms, are believed to be the tombs of the Shimotsukeno clan.<ref>{{Sfn|Fukuda|1998|p=|loc=§. 第三章 下野国成立}}</ref> Nearby, the ruins of the {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}—the clan's temple—further demonstrate their historical significance.<ref>See {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji Ruins|ja|下野薬師寺跡}}.</ref>
== History ==
In {{ill|684|ja|天武天皇13年}}, Emperor Tenmu conferred the title "Ason" on the Shimotsukeno clan as part of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Nobility|ja|八色の姓}}.<ref group="原">Nihon Shoki, Emperor Tenmu, year 13, 11th month.</ref>
Notable members of the clan include {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}}, who contributed to drafting the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, and {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}}, a poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.<ref>{{Sfn|国史大辞典編集委員会|1986|p=|loc=§. 下毛野氏}}</ref> Over time, the clan's influence waned, and they served as middle- and low-ranking court officials.<ref>{{Sfn|kotobank-下毛野氏|loc=世界大百科事典}}</ref>
== Notable Members == * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Komaro|ja|下毛野古麻呂}} - Compiler of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}} - Deputy general in the conquest of the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. * {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Mushimaro|ja|下毛野虫麻呂}} - Poet featured in the {{ill|Kaifūsō|ja|懐風藻}}.
